--- title: "Lab 5: Bikeshare SpaceTime Prediction" subtitle: "CPLN 5080 - Spring 2026" author: "Indrajeet Chavan and Wil Rantala" date: "April 14, 2026" format: html: code-fold: show code-tools: true toc: true toc-depth: 3 toc-location: left theme: cosmo embed-resources: true editor: visual execute: warning: false message: false --- # INTRODUCTION ## The Rebalancing Challenge in Philadelphia Philadelphia's Indego bike share system faces the same operational challenge as every bike share system: **rebalancing bikes to meet anticipated demand**. Imagine you're an Indego operations manager at 6:00 AM on a Monday morning. You have: - 200 stations across Philadelphia - Limited trucks and staff for moving bikes - 2-3 hours before morning rush hour demand peaks - **The question:** Which stations will run out of bikes by 8:30 AM? This lab will teach you to build predictive models that forecast bike share demand across **space** (different stations) and **time** (different hours) to help solve this operational problem. ## Learning Objectives By the end of this assignment, you will be able to: 1. **Understand panel data structure** for space-time analysis 2. **Create temporal lag variables** to capture demand persistence 3. **Build multiple predictive models** with increasing complexity 4. **Validate models temporally** (train on past, test on future) 5. **Analyze prediction errors** in both space and time 6. **Engineer new features** based on error patterns 7. **Critically evaluate** when prediction errors matter most ## Assignment Structure **Part 1 (In-Class/Together):** Work through Q1 2025 data following this code **Part 2 (HW5 - Teams of 2):** - Download a different quarter of Indego data (Q2, Q3, or Q4 2025) - Apply the same workflow to your quarter - Analyze error patterns in your quarter - Add 2-3 new features to improve the model - Write a brief report on what you learned #SET UP ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} rm(list=ls()) library(tidyverse) library(lubridate) library(sf) library(tigris) library(tidycensus) library(riem) # For Philadelphia weather from ASOS stations library(viridis) # color scales for visualization library(knitr) library(kableExtra) library(patchwork) library(here) options(scipen = 999) # Get rid of scientific notation to make it look tidier ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} plotTheme <- theme_minimal() + theme( plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"), plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10), plot.caption = element_text(size = 8), axis.text.x = element_text(size = 10, angle = 45, hjust = 1), axis.text.y = element_text(size = 10), axis.title = element_text(size = 11, face = "bold"), panel.grid.major = element_line(colour = "#D0D0D0", linewidth = 0.2), panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), axis.ticks = element_blank(), legend.position = "right" ) mapTheme <- theme_void() + theme( plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"), plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10), plot.caption = element_text(size = 8), legend.position = "right", plot.margin = margin(1, 1, 1, 1, "cm"), legend.key.height = unit(1, "cm"), legend.key.width = unit(0.2, "cm") ) palette5 <- c("#eff3ff", "#bdd7e7", "#6baed6", "#3182bd", "#08519c") ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} census_api_key(Sys.getenv("CENSUS_API_KEY")) #census_api_key("9c944699f65b68893c6c529610f655faeec5d440", install = TRUE, overwrite = TRUE) #Use the following code if you're not able to access the key in your environment #census_api_key("yourkeyhere", install=TRUE, overwrite=TRUE) ``` ::: # IMPORT AND PREP DATA ## Load Indego Trip Data (Q1 2025) **Note for HW5:** You'll download a different quarter from: https://www.rideindego.com/about/data/ ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Read Q1 2025 data indego <- read_csv(here("data/indego-trips-2025-q1.csv")) # Quick look at the data glimpse(indego) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Rows: 201,588 Columns: 15 $ trip_id 1123985990, 1123986350, 1124202498, 1123986241, 11… $ duration 5, 14, 894, 9, 13, 11, 15, 19, 20, 24, 26, 11, 12,… $ start_time "1/1/2025 0:00", "1/1/2025 0:04", "1/1/2025 0:05",… $ end_time "1/1/2025 0:05", "1/1/2025 0:18", "1/1/2025 14:59"… $ start_station 3371, 3344, 3021, 3253, 3182, 3346, 3049, 3112, 31… $ start_lat 39.95340, 39.95961, 39.95390, 39.93074, 39.95081, … $ start_lon -75.15430, -75.23354, -75.16902, -75.18924, -75.16… $ end_station 3378, 3294, 3073, 3253, 3249, 3000, 3100, 3035, 33… $ end_lat 39.95238, 39.95174, 39.96143, 39.93074, 39.95784, … $ end_lon -75.14728, -75.17063, -75.15242, -75.18924, -75.19… $ bike_id "22580", "31417", "31393", "31434", "22872", "1187… $ plan_duration 30, 365, 1, 30, 1, 365, 30, 30, 365, 365, 30, 365,… $ trip_route_category "One Way", "One Way", "One Way", "Round Trip", "On… $ passholder_type "Indego30", "Indego365", "Walk-up", "Indego30", "W… $ bike_type "electric", "electric", "electric", "electric", "e… ``` ::: ::: **Pause here** Look at the data. What do you suspect the coordinate system is? What do you notice about the date and time structure? ## Examine the Data Structure ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # How many trips? cat("Total trips in Q1 2025:", nrow(indego), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Total trips in Q1 2025: 201588 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # Date range cat("Date range:", min(mdy_hm(indego$start_time)), "to", max(mdy_hm(indego$start_time)), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Date range: 1735689600 to 1743464880 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # How many unique stations? cat("Unique start stations:", length(unique(indego$start_station)), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Unique start stations: 265 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # Trip types table(indego$trip_route_category) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` One Way Round Trip 190792 10796 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # Passholder types table(indego$passholder_type) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Day Pass Indego30 Indego365 IndegoFlex Walk-up 5494 94044 91628 3 10419 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # Bike types table(indego$bike_type) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` electric standard 129561 72027 ``` ::: ::: ## Create Time Bins We need to aggregate trips into hourly intervals for our panel data structure. Read up on the lubridate library for more information on the date/time data extraction. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} indego <- indego %>% mutate( # Parse datetime start_datetime = mdy_hm(start_time), end_datetime = mdy_hm(end_time), # Create hourly bins interval60 = floor_date(start_datetime, unit = "hour"), # Extract time features week = week(interval60), month = month(interval60, label = TRUE), dotw = wday(interval60, label = TRUE), hour = hour(interval60), date = as.Date(interval60), # Create useful indicators weekend = ifelse(dotw %in% c("Sat", "Sun"), 1, 0), rush_hour = ifelse(hour %in% c(7, 8, 9, 16, 17, 18), 1, 0) ) # Look at temporal features head(indego %>% select(start_datetime, interval60, week, dotw, hour, weekend)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 6 × 6 start_datetime interval60 week dotw hour weekend 1 2025-01-01 00:00:00 2025-01-01 00:00:00 1 Wed 0 0 2 2025-01-01 00:04:00 2025-01-01 00:00:00 1 Wed 0 0 3 2025-01-01 00:05:00 2025-01-01 00:00:00 1 Wed 0 0 4 2025-01-01 00:05:00 2025-01-01 00:00:00 1 Wed 0 0 5 2025-01-01 00:08:00 2025-01-01 00:00:00 1 Wed 0 0 6 2025-01-01 00:14:00 2025-01-01 00:00:00 1 Wed 0 0 ``` ::: ::: **Question** Explain the interval60 feature to someone else at your table. #EDA OF INDEGO DATA ## Trips Over Time ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Daily trip counts daily_trips <- indego %>% group_by(date) %>% summarize(trips = n()) ggplot(daily_trips, aes(x = date, y = trips)) + geom_line(color = "#3182bd", linewidth = 1) + geom_smooth(se = TRUE, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") + labs( title = "Indego Daily Ridership - Q1 2025", subtitle = "Winter demand patterns in Philadelphia", x = "Date", y = "Daily Trips", caption = "Source: Indego bike share" ) + plotTheme ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](week11_inclass_and_lab5_files/figure-html/trips_over_time-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: **Question:** What patterns do you see? How does ridership change over time? Notice a big spike in Februrary of 2025 (the 14th - not just Valentine's Day)? Was anybody in Philly last Feb? What closed everything and cancelled my PPA class? ... Hint: Fly Eagles Fly Let's investigate...compare the 14th to all other Friday's in the data...what did you find? Keep this in mind for future Feature Engineering. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} fly_eagles_fly <- daily_trips %>% filter(date == "2025-02-14") typical_boring_friday <- indego %>% filter(dotw == "Fri", date != "2025-02-14") %>% group_by(date) %>% summarize(trips = n()) %>% summarize(avg_friday_trips = mean(trips)) print(fly_eagles_fly) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 1 × 2 date trips 1 2025-02-14 4192 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} print(typical_boring_friday) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 1 × 1 avg_friday_trips 1 2319. ``` ::: ::: ## Hourly Patterns ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Average trips by hour and day type hourly_patterns <- indego %>% group_by(hour, weekend) %>% summarize(avg_trips = n() / n_distinct(date)) %>% mutate(day_type = ifelse(weekend == 1, "Weekend", "Weekday")) ggplot(hourly_patterns, aes(x = hour, y = avg_trips, color = day_type)) + geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) + scale_color_manual(values = c("Weekday" = "#08519c", "Weekend" = "#6baed6")) + labs( title = "Average Hourly Ridership Patterns", subtitle = "Clear commute patterns on weekdays", x = "Hour of Day", y = "Average Trips per Hour", color = "Day Type" ) + plotTheme ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](week11_inclass_and_lab5_files/figure-html/hourly_patterns-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: **Question:** When are the peak hours? How do weekends differ from weekdays? ## Top Stations ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Most popular origin stations top_stations <- indego %>% count(start_station, start_lat, start_lon, name = "trips") %>% arrange(desc(trips)) %>% head(20) kable(top_stations, caption = "Top 20 Indego Stations by Trip Origins", format.args = list(big.mark = ",")) %>% kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover")) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}

Med_Inc = B19013_001E, Total_Commuters = B08301_001E, Transit_Commuters = B08301_010E, White_Pop = B02001_002E, Med_Home_Value = B25077_001E ) %>% mutate( Percent_Taking_Transit = (Transit_Commuters / Total_Commuters) * 100, Percent_White = (White_Pop / Total_Pop) * 100 ) %>% st_transform(crs = 4326) #transforming to WGS84 for lat/lon matching, currently in NAD83 ``` ::: {.cell-output + geom_sf(data = philly_census, aes(fill = Med_Inc), color = NA) + scale_fill_viridis( option = "viridis", name = "Median\nIncome", labels = scales::dollar ) + labs( title = "Philadelphia Median 0.25, alpha = 0.6 ) + mapTheme ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](week11_inclass_and_lab5_files/figure-html/map_philly-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: **Question** These data are in WGS84. If you wanted to calculate a spatial distance-based variable, like a buffer, should you use the dataset as it? ## Join Census Data to Stations We'll spatially join census characteristics to each bike station. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Create sf object for stations stations_sf <- indego %>% distinct(start_station, start_lat, start_lon) %>% filter(!is.na(start_lat), !is.na(start_lon)) %>% st_as_sf(coords = c("start_lon", "start_lat"), crs = 4326) # Spatial join to get census tract for each station stations_census <- st_join(stations_sf, philly_census, left = TRUE) %>% st_drop_geometry() # Look at the result - investigate whether all of the stations joined to census data -- according to the map above there are stations in non-residential tracts. stations_for_map <- indego %>% distinct(start_station, start_lat, start_lon) %>% filter(!is.na(start_lat), !is.na(start_lon)) %>% left_join( stations_census %>% select(start_station, Med_Inc), by = "start_station" ) %>% mutate(has_census = !is.na(Med_Inc)) # Add back to trip data indego_census <- indego %>% left_join( stations_census %>% select(start_station, Med_Inc, Percent_Taking_Transit, Percent_White, Total_Pop), by = "start_station" ) # Prepare data for visualization stations_for_map <- indego %>% distinct(start_station, start_lat, start_lon) %>% filter(!is.na(start_lat), !is.na(start_lon)) %>% left_join( stations_census %>% select(start_station, Med_Inc), by = "start_station" ) %>% mutate(has_census = !is.na(Med_Inc)) # Create the map showing problem stations ggplot() + geom_sf(data = philly_census, aes(fill = Med_Inc), color = "white", size = 0.1) + scale_fill_viridis( option = "viridis", name = "Median\nIncome", labels = scales::dollar, na.value = "grey90" ) + # Stations with census data (small grey dots) geom_point( data = stations_for_map %>% filter(has_census), aes(x = start_lon, y = start_lat), color = "grey30", size = 1, alpha = 0.6 ) + # Stations WITHOUT census data (red X marks the spot) geom_point( data = stations_for_map %>% filter(!has_census), aes(x = start_lon, y = start_lat), color = "red", size = 1, shape = 4, stroke = 1.5 ) + labs( title = "Philadelphia Median Household Income by Census Tract", subtitle = "Indego stations shown (RED = no census data match)", caption = "Red X marks indicate stations that didn't join to census tracts" ) + mapTheme ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](week11_inclass_and_lab5_files/figure-html/join_census_to_stations-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ## Dealing with missing data We need to decide what to do with the non-residential bike share stations. For this example, we are going to remove them -- this is not necessarily the right way to do things always, but for the sake of simplicity, we are narrowing our scope to only stations in residential neighborhoods. We might opt to create a separate model for non-residential stations.. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Identify which stations to keep valid_stations <- stations_census %>% filter(!is.na(Med_Inc)) %>% pull(start_station) # Filter trip data to valid stations only indego_census <- indego %>% filter(start_station %in% valid_stations) %>% left_join( stations_census %>% select(start_station, Med_Inc, Percent_Taking_Transit, Percent_White, Total_Pop), by = "start_station" ) ``` ::: ## Get Weather Data Weather significantly affects bike share demand! Let's get hourly weather for Philadelphia. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Get weather from Philadelphia International Airport (KPHL) # This covers Q1 2025: January 1 - March 31 weather_data <- riem_measures( station = "PHL", # Philadelphia International Airport date_start = "2025-01-01", date_end = "2025-03-31" ) # Process weather data weather_processed <- weather_data %>% mutate( interval60 = floor_date(valid, unit = "hour"), Temperature = tmpf, # Temperature in Fahrenheit Precipitation = ifelse(is.na(p01i), 0, p01i), # Hourly precip in inches Wind_Speed = sknt # Wind speed in knots ) %>% select(interval60, Temperature, Precipitation, Wind_Speed) %>% distinct(interval60, .keep_all = TRUE) # Check for missing hours and interpolate if needed weather_complete <- weather_processed %>% complete(interval60 = seq(min(interval60), max(interval60), by = "hour")) %>% fill(Temperature, Precipitation, Wind_Speed, .direction = "down") # Look at the weather summary(weather_complete %>% select(Temperature, Precipitation, Wind_Speed)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Temperature Precipitation Wind_Speed Min. :10.00 Min. :0.000000 Min. : 0.000 1st Qu.:30.00 1st Qu.:0.000000 1st Qu.: 6.000 Median :37.00 Median :0.000000 Median : 8.000 Mean :38.36 Mean :0.001327 Mean : 9.308 3rd Qu.:46.00 3rd Qu.:0.000000 3rd Qu.:13.000 Max. :78.00 Max. :0.280000 Max. :30.000 ``` ::: ::: ## Visualize Weather Patterns Who is glad it's Spring!? ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(weather_complete, aes(x = interval60, y = Temperature)) + geom_line(color = "#3182bd", alpha = 0.7) + geom_smooth(se = FALSE, color = "red") + labs( title = "Philadelphia Temperature - Q1 2025", subtitle = "Winter to early spring transition", x = "Date", y = "Temperature (°F)" ) + plotTheme ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](week11_inclass_and_lab5_files/figure-html/visualize-weather-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: # CREATE SPACE-TIME PANEL ## Aggregate Trips to Station-Hour Level ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Count trips by station-hour trips_panel <- indego_census %>% group_by(interval60, start_station, start_lat, start_lon, Med_Inc, Percent_Taking_Transit, Percent_White, Total_Pop) %>% summarize(Trip_Count = n()) %>% ungroup() # How many station-hour observations? nrow(trips_panel) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 116718 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # How many unique stations? length(unique(trips_panel$start_station)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 245 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # How many unique hours? length(unique(trips_panel$interval60)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] 2150 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} hist(trips_panel$Trip_Count) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](week11_inclass_and_lab5_files/figure-html/aggregate-trips-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ## Create Complete Panel Structure Not every station has trips every hour. We need a **complete panel** where every station-hour combination exists (even if Trip_Count = 0). ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Calculate expected panel size n_stations <- length(unique(trips_panel$start_station)) n_hours <- length(unique(trips_panel$interval60)) expected_rows <- n_stations * n_hours cat("Expected panel rows:", format(expected_rows, big.mark = ","), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Expected panel rows: 526,750 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cat("Current rows:", format(nrow(trips_panel), big.mark = ","), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Current rows: 116,718 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cat("Missing rows:", format(expected_rows - nrow(trips_panel), big.mark = ","), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Missing rows: 410,032 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # Create complete panel study_panel <- expand_grid( interval60 = unique(trips_panel$interval60), start_station = unique(trips_panel$start_station) ) %>% # Join trip counts left_join(trips_panel %>% select(interval60, start_station, Trip_Count), by = c("interval60", "start_station")) %>% # Replace NA trip counts If there were 15 bike trips from Station A at 8:00 AM, there will probably be \~15 trips at 9:00 AM. We can use this temporal persistence to improve predictions. **Note:** We still can't use spatial lags in prediction. Temporal lags are different because time moves in one direction - past predicts the future, but the future does not predict the past. Spatial lags are non-directional and create circular prediction logic. Temporal lags: good Spatial lags: bad! ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Sort by station and time study_panel <- study_panel %>% arrange(start_station, interval60) # Create lag variables WITHIN each station study_panel <- study_panel %>% group_by(start_station) %>% mutate( lag1Hour = lag(Trip_Count, 1), lag2Hours = lag(Trip_Count, 2), lag3Hours = lag(Trip_Count, 3), lag12Hours = lag(Trip_Count, 12), lag1day = lag(Trip_Count, 24) ) %>% ungroup() # Remove rows with NA lags (first 24 hours for each station) study_panel_complete <- study_panel %>% filter(!is.na(lag1day)) cat("Rows after removing NA lags:", format(nrow(study_panel_complete), big.mark = ","), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Rows after removing NA lags: 520,870 ``` ::: ::: ## Visualize Lag Correlations ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Sample one station to visualize example_station <- study_panel_complete %>% filter(start_station == first(start_station)) %>% head(168) # One week # Plot actual vs lagged demand ggplot(example_station, aes(x = interval60)) + geom_line(aes(y = Trip_Count, color = "Current"), linewidth = 1) + geom_line(aes(y = lag1Hour, color = "1 Hour Ago"), linewidth = 1, alpha = 0.7) + geom_line(aes(y = lag1day, color = "24 Hours Ago"), linewidth = 1, alpha = 0.7) + scale_color_manual(values = c( "Current" = "#08519c", "1 Hour Ago" = "#3182bd", "24 Hours Ago" = "#6baed6" )) + labs( title = "Temporal Lag Patterns at One Station", subtitle = "Past demand predicts future demand", x = "Date-Time", y = "Trip Count", color = "Time Period" ) + plotTheme ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](week11_inclass_and_lab5_files/figure-html/lag_correlations-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: # TEMPORAL TRAIN/TEST SPLIT **CRITICAL:** We must train on PAST data and test on FUTURE data! ## Why Temporal Validation Matters In real operations, at 6:00 AM on March 15, we need to predict demand for March 15-31. We have data from Jan 1 - March 14, but NOT from March 15-31 (it hasn't happened yet!). **Wrong approach:** Train on weeks 10-13, test on weeks 1-9 (predicting past from future!) **Correct approach:** Train on weeks 1-9, test on weeks 10-13 (predicting future from past) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Split by week # Q1 has weeks 1-13 (Jan-Mar) # Train on weeks 1-9 (Jan 1 - early March) # Test on weeks 10-13 (rest of March) # Which stations have trips in BOTH early and late periods? early_stations <- study_panel_complete %>% filter(week < 10) %>% filter(Trip_Count > 0) %>% distinct(start_station) %>% pull(start_station) late_stations <- study_panel_complete %>% filter(week >= 10) %>% filter(Trip_Count > 0) %>% distinct(start_station) %>% pull(start_station) # Keep only stations that appear in BOTH periods common_stations <- intersect(early_stations, late_stations) # Filter panel to only common stations study_panel_complete <- study_panel_complete %>% filter(start_station %in% common_stations) # NOW create train/test split train <- study_panel_complete %>% filter(week < 10) test <- study_panel_complete %>% filter(week >= 10) cat("Training observations:", format(nrow(train), big.mark = ","), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Training observations: 352,240 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cat("Testing observations:", format(nrow(test), big.mark = ","), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Testing observations: 153,748 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cat("Training date range:", min(train$date), "to", max(train$date), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Training date range: 20090 to 20151 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cat("Testing date range:", min(test$date), "to", max(test$date), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Testing date range: 20152 to 20178 ``` ::: ::: #BUILD PREDICTIVE MODELS We'll build 5 models with increasing complexity to see what improves predictions. ## Model 1: Baseline (Time + Weather) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Create day of week factor with treatment (dummy) coding train <- train %>% mutate(dotw_simple = factor(dotw, levels = c("Mon", "Tue", "Wed", "Thu", "Fri", "Sat", "Sun"))) # Set contrasts to treatment coding (dummy variables) contrasts(train$dotw_simple) <- contr.treatment(7) # Now run the model model1 <- lm( Trip_Count ~ as.factor(hour) + dotw_simple + Temperature + Precipitation, data = train ) summary(model1) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Call: lm(formula = Trip_Count ~ as.factor(hour) + dotw_simple + Temperature + Precipitation, data = train) Residuals: Min 1Q Median 3Q Max -0.9505 -0.4005 -0.1795 0.0221 18.5359 Coefficients: Estimate Std. The second, greater peak is between 4pm and 7pm, with a max average of over 450 trips/hr around 5pm. Weekends, on the other hand, have a plateau'd peak from 12pm until around 6pm, staying consistent around 260 trips/hr. The dual peak pattern for weekdays reflects the morning commute rush and much greater PM after-work rush when many go home, go shopping, or travel to recreational activities. The plateau peak of trips on weekends reflects the less work-oriented travel patterns of Philadelphia on weekends. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Most popular origin stations top_stations <- indegoQ2 %>% count(start_station, start_lat, start_lon, name = "trips") %>% arrange(desc(trips)) %>% head(20) kable(top_stations, caption = "Top 20 Indego Stations by Trip Origins", format.args = list(big.mark = ",")) %>% kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover")) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}

prediction: past demand predicts future demand. Why Temporal Lags? If there were 15 bike trips from Station A at 8:00 AM, there will probably be ~15 trips at 9:00 AM. We can use this temporal persistence to improve predictions. Note: We still can't use spatial lags in prediction. Temporal lags are different because time moves in one direction - past predicts the future, but the future does not predict the past. Spatial lags are non-directional and create circular prediction logic. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Sort by station and time study_panelQ2 <- study_panelQ2 %>% arrange(start_station, interval60) # Create lag variables WITHIN each station study_panelQ2 <- study_panelQ2 %>% group_by(start_station) %>% mutate( lag1Hour = lag(Trip_Count, 1), lag2Hours = lag(Trip_Count, 2), lag3Hours = lag(Trip_Count, 3), lag12Hours = lag(Trip_Count, 12), lag1day = lag(Trip_Count, 24) ) %>% ungroup() # Remove rows with NA lags (first 24 hours for each station) study_panel_completeQ2 <- study_panelQ2 %>% filter(!is.na(lag1day)) cat("Rows after removing NA lags:", format(nrow(study_panel_completeQ2), big.mark = ","), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Rows after removing NA lags: 542,556 ``` ::: ::: ##Visualize Lag Correlations ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Sample one station to visualize example_station <- study_panel_completeQ2 %>% filter(start_station == first(start_station)) %>% head(168) # One week # Plot actual vs lagged demand ggplot(example_station, aes(x = interval60)) + geom_line(aes(y = Trip_Count, color = "Current"), linewidth = 1) + geom_line(aes(y = lag1Hour, color = "1 Hour Ago"), linewidth = 1, alpha = 0.7) + geom_line(aes(y = lag1day, color = "24 Hours Ago"), linewidth = 1, alpha = 0.7) + scale_color_manual(values = c( "Current" = "#08519c", "1 Hour Ago" = "#3182bd", "24 Hours Ago" = "#6baed6" )) + labs( title = "Temporal Lag Patterns at One Station", subtitle = "Past demand predicts future demand", x = "Date-Time", y = "Trip Count", color = "Time Period" ) + plotTheme ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](week11_inclass_and_lab5_files/figure-html/lag_correlations Q2-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ## TEMPORAL TRAIN/TEST SPLIT CRITICAL: We must train on PAST data and test on FUTURE data! Why Temporal Validation Matters In real operations, at 6:00 AM on June 15, we need to predict demand for June 15-31. We have data from April 1 - June 14, but NOT from June 15-31 (it hasn't happened yet!). Wrong approach: Train on weeks 10-13, test on weeks 1-9 (predicting past from future!) Correct approach: Train on weeks 1-9, test on weeks 10-13 (predicting future from past) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Split by week # Q2 has weeks 1-13 (Apr-Jun) # Train on weeks 1-9 (Apr 1 - early June) # Test on weeks 10-13 (rest of June) # Which stations have trips in BOTH early and late periods? early_stationsQ2 <- study_panel_completeQ2 %>% filter(week < 23) %>% filter(Trip_Count > 0) %>% distinct(start_station) %>% pull(start_station) late_stationsQ2 <- study_panel_completeQ2 %>% filter(week >= 23) %>% filter(Trip_Count > 0) %>% distinct(start_station) %>% pull(start_station) # Keep only stations that appear in BOTH periods common_stationsQ2 <- intersect(early_stationsQ2, late_stationsQ2) # Filter panel to only common stations study_panel_completeQ2 <- study_panel_completeQ2 %>% filter(start_station %in% common_stationsQ2) # NOW create train/test split trainQ2 <- study_panel_completeQ2 %>% filter(week < 23) testQ2 <- study_panel_completeQ2 %>% filter(week >= 23) cat("Training observations:", format(nrow(trainQ2), big.mark = ","), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Training observations: 374,745 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cat("Testing observations:", format(nrow(testQ2), big.mark = ","), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Testing observations: 161,352 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cat("Training date range:", min(trainQ2$date), "to", max(trainQ2$date), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Training date range: 20180 to 20242 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cat("Testing date range:", min(testQ2$date), "to", max(testQ2$date), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Testing date range: 20243 to 20269 ``` ::: ::: ## BUILD PREDICTIVE MODELS We'll build 5 models with increasing complexity to see what improves predictions. ##Model 1: Baseline (Time + Weather) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Create day of week factor with treatment (dummy) coding trainQ2 <- trainQ2 %>% mutate(dotw_simple = factor(dotw, levels = c("Mon", "Tue", "Wed", "Thu", "Fri", "Sat", "Sun"))) # Set contrasts to treatment coding (dummy variables) contrasts(trainQ2$dotw_simple) <- contr.treatment(7) # Now run the model model1Q2 <- lm( Trip_Count ~ as.factor(hour) + dotw_simple + Temperature + Precipitation, data = trainQ2 ) summary(model1Q2) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Call: lm(formula = Trip_Count ~ as.factor(hour) + dotw_simple + Temperature + Precipitation, data = trainQ2) Residuals: Min 1Q Median 3Q Max -1.6562 -0.6692 -0.2075 0.1903 25.4970 Coefficients: Estimate Std. What do station fixed effects capture? Baseline differences in demand across stations (some are just busier than others!). ##Model 5: Add Rush Hour Interaction ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} model5Q2 <- lm( Trip_Count ~ as.factor(hour) + dotw_simple + Temperature + Precipitation + lag1Hour + lag3Hours + lag1day + rush_hour + as.factor(month) + Med_Inc + Percent_Taking_Transit + Percent_White + as.factor(start_station) + rush_hour * weekend, # Rush hour effects different on weekends data = trainQ2 ) cat("Model 5 R-squared:", summary(model5Q2)$r.squared, "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Model 5 R-squared: 0.3407678 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cat("Model 5 Adj R-squared:", summary(model5Q2)$adj.r.squared, "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Model 5 Adj R-squared: 0.3402661 ``` ::: ::: Our 5th model with rush hour interactions added is our best-fitting one so far. With an adjusted R^2 of 0.340, it accounts for 34% of the variation in trip counts at the station-hour. That being said, adding the rush hour interaction only improved the R^2 by 0.04 (or 4%). ##MODEL EVALUATION Calculate Predictions and MAE ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Get predictions on test set # Create day of week factor with treatment (dummy) coding testQ2 <- testQ2 %>% mutate(dotw_simple = factor(dotw, levels = c("Mon", "Tue", "Wed", "Thu", "Fri", "Sat", "Sun"))) # Set contrasts to treatment coding (dummy variables) contrasts(testQ2$dotw_simple) <- contr.treatment(7) testQ2 <- testQ2 %>% mutate( pred1Q2 = predict(model1Q2, newdata = testQ2), pred2Q2 = predict(model2Q2, newdata = testQ2), pred3Q2 = predict(model3Q2, newdata = testQ2), pred4Q2 = predict(model4Q2, newdata = testQ2), pred5Q2 = predict(model5Q2, newdata = testQ2) ) # Calculate MAE for each model mae_resultsQ2 <- data.frame( Model = c( "1. Time + Weather", "2. + Temporal Lags", "3. + Demographics", "4. + Station FE", "5. + Rush Hour Interaction" ), MAE = c( mean(abs(testQ2$Trip_Count - testQ2$pred1Q2), na.rm = TRUE), mean(abs(testQ2$Trip_Count - testQ2$pred2Q2), na.rm = TRUE), mean(abs(testQ2$Trip_Count - testQ2$pred3Q2), na.rm = TRUE), mean(abs(testQ2$Trip_Count - testQ2$pred4Q2), na.rm = TRUE), mean(abs(testQ2$Trip_Count - testQ2$pred5Q2), na.rm = TRUE) ) ) kable(mae_resultsQ2, digits = 2, caption = "Mean Absolute Error by Model (Test Set)", col.names = c("Model", "MAE (trips)")) %>% kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover")) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}

The Rush hour interaction actually worsened the MAE from 0.69 to 0.71. # Part 2: Error Analysis Analyze your model's errors in detail: 1. **Spatial patterns:** - Create error maps - Identify neighborhoods with high errors - Hypothesize why (missing features? different demand patterns?) 2. **Temporal patterns:** - When are errors highest? - Do certain hours/days have systematic under/over-prediction? - Are there seasonal patterns? 3. **Demographic patterns:** - Relate errors to census characteristics - Are certain communities systematically harder to predict? - What are the equity implications? ## SPACE-TIME ERROR ANALYSIS ## Observed vs. Predicted Let's use our best model (Model 4) for error analysis. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} testQ2 <- testQ2 %>% mutate( error = Trip_Count - pred4Q2, abs_error = abs(error), time_of_day = case_when( hour < 7 ~ "Overnight", hour >= 7 & hour < 10 ~ "AM Rush", hour >= 10 & hour < 15 ~ "Mid-Day", hour >= 15 & hour <= 18 ~ "PM Rush", hour > 18 ~ "Evening" ) ) # Scatter plot by time and day type ggplot(testQ2, aes(x = Trip_Count, y = pred4Q2)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.2, color = "#3182bd") + geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, color = "red", linewidth = 1) + geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "darkgreen") + facet_grid(weekend ~ time_of_day) + labs( title = "Observed vs. Predicted Bike Trips", subtitle = "Model 4 performance by time period", x = "Observed Trips", y = "Predicted Trips", caption = "Red line = perfect predictions; Green line = actual model fit" ) + plotTheme ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](week11_inclass_and_lab5_files/figure-html/obs_vs_pred Q2-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: Question: Where is the model performing well? Under what conditions? The Poisson model shows a clear improvement in fit, with a substantial reduction in deviance and an MAE of ~0.75 trips. Whether this is “good enough” depends on station-level demand. But for low-demand stations, this level of error may be acceptable, but for high-demand stations or peak hours, even small absolute errors can translate into meaningful operational issues. The strong and highly significant coefficient on lag1Hour (z = 372) suggests that short-term demand persistence is the dominant driver, which is useful for near-term forecasting but may limit performance during sudden shifts. Prediction errors are most problematic during peak demand periods and atypical conditions. For example, underestimating demand during rush hours can lead to empty stations, while overestimation during low-demand periods can result in inefficient truck dispatching. Given these dynamics, this model is suitable as a decision-support tool rather than a fully automated system. It would be most effective when paired with real-time monitoring and used for short-term forecasting like the next hour, particularly in stable demand conditions. 2. **Equity considerations:** - Do prediction errors disproportionately affect certain neighborhoods? - Could this system worsen existing disparities in bike access? - What safeguards would you recommend? Prediction errors are unlikely to be evenly distributed across space. Since the model relies heavily on historical demand by using the variable lag1Hour and general temporal/weather patterns, it will tend to perform better in high-usage, well-established stations and worse in low-demand or historically underserved neighborhoods. This creates a risk where rebalancing decisions reinforce existing usage patterns—allocating more bikes to already busy areas while neglecting communities with lower baseline demand. To prevent this, safeguards should include monitoring model error geographically, setting minimum service thresholds across all neighborhoods, and incorporating equity-weighted objectives into optimization decisions. For example, ensuring a baseline number of bikes in underserved areas regardless of predicted demand can help avoid reinforcing disparities. 3. **Model limitations:** - What patterns is your model missing? - What assumptions might not hold in real deployment? - How would you improve this with more time/data? While the model captures key temporal and weather effects, it misses several important patterns. It assumes relatively stable relationships between predictors and demand, which may not hold during special events, system disruptions, or behavioral shifts. The negative coefficient on perfect_bike_weather is a sign that some nonlinear or interaction effects are not being fully captured, suggesting model misspecification. Additionally, the model lacks fine-grained spatial features like proximity to transit, land use and bike infrastructure and does not account for real-time system dynamics such as bike availability or station capacity constraints. With more time and data, performance could be improved by incorporating: - richer spatial variables (e.g., distance to Center City, bike lanes), - event and calendar data, - and more flexible models (e.g., gradient boosting or spatiotemporal models) that better capture non-linearities and localized variation. ------------------------------------------------------------------------ # Submission Requirements ## What to Submit (per team) 1. **qmd file** with all your code (commented!) 2. **Brief report** summarizing the following with supporting data & visualization: - Your quarter and why you chose it - Model comparison results - Error analysis insights - New features you added and why - Critical reflection on deployment 3. **HTML output** with results and visualizations ## Tips for Success 1. **Start early** - data download and processing takes time 2. **Work together** - pair programming is your friend 3. **Test incrementally** - don't wait until the end to run code 4. **Document everything** - explain your choices 5. **Be creative** - the best features come from understanding Philly! 6. **Think critically** - technical sophistication isn't enough ------------------------------------------------------------------------
